_note: Data-modeling checkpoints that are not domain-specific.

checkpoints.title: Money stored and computed safely
checkpoints.why: Floating-point money causes rounding errors that compound across totals, tax, and refunds.

levels.3: Prices as floats; arithmetic in floating point.
levels.6: Integer minor units (cents) or decimal type throughout; consistent rounding.
levels.9: Minor-unit integers, explicit currency per amount, centralized money type, rounding rules tested.

verify.reasoning: How is money represented in schema and calculations? Are floats used in price/tax/total math? Is currency explicit on each amount?

assertions.check: No float type backs price/total/tax fields.
Currency stored alongside every monetary amount.

checkpoints.title: Personal data is minimized, protected, and deletable
checkpoints.why: Apps quietly accumulate personal data (emails, addresses, phone, IPs) with no plan for it - collected beyond need, spilled into logs or over-broad API responses, kept forever, with no way to delete or export it. That is both a breach blast-radius multiplier and, in many places, a legal obligation (GDPR/CCPA) that AI-generated code never accounts for.

levels.3: Personal data collected without intent, returned in broad API responses or written to logs, kept indefinitely, with no deletion/export path.
levels.6: Only needed personal data is collected; it is kept out of logs and out of over-broad responses; a user-deletion (and export) path exists.
levels.9: Documented data inventory + retention windows, sensitive fields encrypted at rest, access scoped and audited, and tested deletion/export that honors linked records.

verify.reasoning: What personal data does the app store, and is any of it beyond what the feature needs? Does personal data leak into logs or wide API responses? Is there a retention limit and a way for a user to delete or export their data?

assertions.check: Personal data is not written to logs or returned in over-broad API responses.
A path exists to delete (and export) a user's personal data.

checkpoints.title: Queries are bounded and free of N+1 patterns
checkpoints.why: AI-generated data access commonly issues a query per row (N+1) and lists records with no pagination or limit. It looks fine on ten rows and falls over in production - slow pages, exhausted connection pools, memory blowups - and an unbounded list endpoint is a cheap denial-of-service.

levels.3: Queries issued inside loops (N+1); list/collection endpoints fetch all rows with no limit or pagination.
levels.6: Related data loaded in bulk (joins/batched/`include`), and list endpoints are paginated or bounded by a sane maximum.
levels.9: Hot paths backed by appropriate indexes, cursor pagination on large sets, and query counts/latency watched so regressions surface.

verify.reasoning: Are there queries executed inside loops (a query per item) where a single batched or joined query would do? Do list/collection endpoints paginate or cap results, or can they return unbounded rows?

assertions.check: No per-row query inside a loop where a batched/joined query would do.
List/collection endpoints are paginated or bounded by a maximum.
